title: Quiz: Black-Scholes Model Fundamentals
description: Test your understanding of Black-Scholes option pricing

questions.question: What does N(d₁) represent in the Black-Scholes formula for a call option?

options.text: The probability the option expires in the money
The delta of the option (hedge ratio)

questions.explanation: N(d₁) represents the delta of a call option - the number of shares needed to delta-hedge one call. It's the sensitivity of the option price to changes in the underlying.

questions.question: Which Black-Scholes assumption is violated by the 'volatility smile' observed in markets?
questions.explanation: The volatility smile shows that implied volatility varies with strike price, violating the Black-Scholes assumption of constant volatility across all strikes.

questions.question: How does an increase in volatility affect option prices?

options.text: Call prices increase, put prices decrease
Call prices decrease, put prices increase
Both call and put prices increase
Both call and put prices decrease

questions.explanation: Higher volatility increases both call and put prices because options have asymmetric payoffs - they benefit from large moves in either direction while limiting losses to the premium paid.

questions.question: What is put-call parity for European options?
questions.explanation: Put-call parity states C - P = S - Ke^(-rT), or equivalently C + Ke^(-rT) = P + S. This relationship must hold to prevent arbitrage.

questions.question: Black-Scholes can be used to accurately price American put options.
questions.explanation: False. Black-Scholes is designed for European options only. American puts can be exercised early, which may be optimal, making Black-Scholes prices inaccurate. Binomial trees or other methods are needed.

questions.question: What happens to a call option price when interest rates increase (all else equal)?
questions.explanation: Higher interest rates increase call prices because the present value of the strike payment decreases (Ke^(-rT) is smaller), making the call more valuable.

questions.question: How does a continuous dividend yield affect call option prices?
questions.explanation: Dividend payments reduce the stock price on ex-dividend dates, making call options less valuable. In Black-Scholes, we multiply S by e^(-qT) to account for this.

questions.question: Which are valid methods for calculating implied volatility? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Implied volatility must be found numerically using root-finding methods like bisection, Newton-Raphson, or Brent's method. There is no closed-form solution that inverts Black-Scholes for σ.
